topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the schematics spread across the worn oak table. Her breath hitched, a shallow, rapid intake of air that did little to settle the churning in her stomach. The blue ink lines, the precise measurements of the chronometer assembly—they were identical to the notes she had taken three days ago, notes she distinctly remembered writing alone in her cramped workshop. A cold knot tightened in her chest, spreading outward like an icy tide. She frantically flipped through the physical prototypes resting beside the blueprints, each component—the brushed aluminum casing, the delicate wiring—mirroring the ghostly images in her mind. "No," she whispered, the sound brittle in the quiet room. She snatched up her phone, her fingers trembling so violently she nearly dropped it, and began typing a frantic message to Marcus, the only other person she knew who shared this obscure research interest. Every keystroke felt heavy, each pause an agonizing stretch of time.

[story 2]

Liam dropped the soldering iron. It clattered onto the workbench with a sharp, startling sound that seemed deafening in the otherwise silent laboratory. He blinked hard, trying to clear the sudden fog creeping over his vision. The complex circuit diagram illuminated under the harsh overhead lamp was undeniably the same one he had spent the last fortnight agonizing over. He traced a finger over a specific junction point, feeling a distinct, unwelcome tremor run up his arm. His heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out all other thought. He looked up, scanning the room, searching for an explanation, a misplaced document, anything to account for the impossible duplication. "This can't be right," he muttered, his voice tight and strained. He pushed back from the table, his movements jerky and uncoordinated, needing to move, needing air, needing to escape the suffocating symmetry of the situation.

[story 3]

Across the vast drafting table, Clara felt the blood drain from her face, leaving her skin clammy and cold. The finished prototype, gleaming under the fluorescent lights, was an exact replica of the one she had just finished assembling hours earlier. Her hands, usually steady when handling micro-components, began to shake uncontrollably, making it difficult to hold the tool steady. A wave of sheer disorientation washed over her; the world seemed to tilt slightly on its axis. She squeezed her eyes shut, trying to force the image away, but the identical texture of the casing, the precise arrangement of the internal mechanisms, refused to leave her sight. A low, involuntary moan escaped her lips. She needed to verify, needed proof, but every instinct screamed that the reality before her was fundamentally, terrifyingly wrong.
